en.extract:
The mottled owl (Strix virgata) is a medium-sized owl found in Central and South America from Mexico to Brazil and Argentina. The head and back are mottled brown and the underparts whitish, with vertical bars on the chest and throat. The eyes are dark and the head is round and they do not have ear tufts. They are territorial and found in dry forests and jungles at altitudes of up to 2,500 m (8,200 ft) above sea level.


Taxonomy
The mottled owl was described by the American ornithologist John Cassin in 1849 and given the binomial name Syrnium virgatum. In 1999, Wink and Heidrich transferred it to the genus Strix, but this is still contested by some authorities.


Description

The mottled owl is a medium-sized owl with adults reaching 280 to 355 mm (11 to 14 in) in length. Females are considerably larger than males; the mottled owl shows the greatest degree of sexual dimorphism of any species of owl. The crown, nape and back are mottled in several shades of darkish brown, the facial disc is pale brown and the throat, breast and belly are off-white with distinctive vertical brown streaks. The large eyes are brown, the beak is greyish-yellow or greyish-blue, and the legs and feet are greyish-yellow. There is a darker form of the bird with a buff breast and belly. Mottled owls produce a range of calls which include a hoot used in maintaining territory boundaries, and various whistles, screeches and hisses.


Distribution and habitat
The mottled owl is native to Central and South America. Its range extends from Mexico south to Argentina and Brazil and it is found at elevations up to about 2,500 m (8,200 ft). It inhabits a variety of wooded habitats including rainforest, woodland verges, dry thorn forest, pine–oak woodland and plantations and also open countryside with scattered trees. In some parts of its range it is common and it is often found close to human habitations.  In 1983, a specimen was retrieved on the American side of the Rio Grande, thus establishing a first record for the United States.  Forty years later, a mottled owl was photographed in Starr County, Texas, and there is also a 2006 record from the same state.


Behaviour
The mottled owl is nocturnal and spends the day in dense vegetation where it may be mobbed by other birds. Its large eyes are adapted for sight at low levels of light, and its hearing is also acute. It is a predator and at night often perches on a branch beside a glade or at the edge of woodland on the lookout for prey. When it detects a small moving object, it swoops down from its perch on silent wings and pounces on its target, which may be a small mammal, a bird, reptile or amphibian or a large beetle, grasshopper or other insect/arthropod. It is known to prey on bats.
The mottled owl breeds between February and May in Colombia and in September to November in Argentina. It usually nests in a hole in a tree but may also choose an empty nest built by another species. One or usually two white eggs are laid and incubated by the female and both parents care for the young.


Status
The mottled owl has a very wide range and the number of individual birds has been estimated to be somewhere between five hundred thousand and five million. It is listed by the IUCN as being of "Least Concern" on the basis that, though its numbers may be decreasing slightly, they are not doing so at such a rate as would justify putting the bird in a more vulnerable category.


References


External links

"Mottled Owl (Strix virgata) - Picture 2 in Strix: Virgata". Mottled Owl photo at Lewis, Deane. "The Owl Pages - Information about Owls". The Owl Pages. Retrieved 24 May 2025. owlpages.com
"Mottled owl media". Internet Bird Collection.
Mottled owl photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)
Mottled owl species account at Neotropical Birds (Cornell Lab of Ornithology)
Interactive range map of Ciccaba virgata at IUCN Red List

The '''mottled owl''' ('''''Strix virgata''''') is a medium-sized [[owl]] found in Central and South America from [[Mexico]] to [[Brazil]] and [[Argentina]]. The head and back are mottled brown and the underparts whitish, with vertical bars on the chest and throat. The eyes are dark and the head is round and they do not have ear tufts. They are territorial and found in dry forests and jungles at altitudes of up to {{cvt|2500|m|ft|-1}} above sea level.

==Taxonomy==
The mottled owl was [[Species description|described]] by the American ornithologist [[John Cassin]] in 1849 and given the [[binomial name]] ''Syrnium virgatum''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last= Cassin | first=John | author-link=John Cassin | year=1849 | title=Species of owls, presumed to be undescribed, specimens of which are in the collection of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia | journal=Proceedings of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia | volume=4 | pages=121–125 [124] |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/5445387 }} The title page is dated 1848 but the volume was not published until the following year.</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1940 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=4 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=154 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14476625 }}</ref> In 1999, Wink and Heidrich transferred it to the [[genus]] ''[[Strix (genus)|Strix]]'', but this is still contested by some authorities.<ref name=SACC/>

==Description==
[[Image: Mottled_Owl.png |left|thumb| Chan Chich Lodge area – Belize]]
The mottled owl is a medium-sized owl with adults reaching {{cvt|280|to|355|mm|in|0}} in length. Females are considerably larger than males; the mottled owl shows the greatest degree of [[sexual dimorphism]] of any species of owl.<ref name=ADW/> The crown, nape and back are mottled in several shades of darkish brown, the facial disc is pale brown and the throat, breast and belly are off-white with distinctive vertical brown streaks. The large eyes are brown, the beak is greyish-yellow or greyish-blue, and the legs and feet are greyish-yellow. There is a darker form of the bird with a buff breast and belly. Mottled owls produce a range of calls which include a hoot used in maintaining [[Territory (animal)|territory]] boundaries, and various whistles, screeches and hisses.<ref name=ADW/><ref name=Konig/>

==Distribution and habitat==
The mottled owl is native to Central and South America. Its range extends from Mexico south to Argentina and Brazil and it is found at elevations up to about {{cvt|2500|m|ft|-1}}. It inhabits a variety of wooded habitats including rainforest, woodland verges, dry thorn forest, [[pine–oak woodland]] and plantations and also open countryside with scattered trees. In some parts of its range it is common and it is often found close to human habitations.<ref name=ADW/>  In 1983, a specimen was retrieved on the American side of the Rio Grande, thus establishing a first record for the United States.  Forty years later, a mottled owl was photographed in Starr County, Texas, and there is also a 2006 record from the same state.<ref>{{cite web|title=Checklist – 21 Nov 2023 – Santa Margarita Ranch (LTC 079) |url=https://ebird.org/checklist/S154949141 |website=eBird|date=21 November 2023}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.aba.org/rare-bird-alert-december-1-2023/ |title=Rare Bird Alert: December 1, 2023|website=aba American Birding Association|date=1 December 2023}}</ref>

==Behaviour==
The mottled owl is [[nocturnal]] and spends the day in dense vegetation where it may be mobbed by other birds. Its large eyes are adapted for sight at low levels of light, and its hearing is also acute. It is a [[Predation|predator]] and at night often perches on a branch beside a glade or at the edge of woodland on the lookout for prey. When it detects a small moving object, it swoops down from its perch on silent wings and pounces on its target, which may be a small mammal, a bird, reptile or amphibian or a large beetle, grasshopper or other insect/arthropod.<ref name=ADW/><ref>{{cite book|author=Proudfoot, Glenn|year=2011|title=Owls of the World |edition=2nd|editor=Claus König|editor2=Friedhelm Weick|publisher=Yale University Press |location=New Haven, Connecticut}}</ref> It is known to prey on [[bat]]s.<ref name=Brown/>

The mottled owl breeds between February and May in Colombia and in September to November in Argentina. It usually nests in a hole in a tree but may also choose an empty nest built by another species. One or usually two white eggs are laid and incubated by the female and both parents care for the young.<ref name=Konig/>

==Status==
The mottled owl has a very wide range and the number of individual birds has been estimated to be somewhere between five hundred thousand and five million. It is listed by the [[IUCN]] as being of "[[Least Concern]]" on the basis that, though its numbers may be decreasing slightly, they are not doing so at such a rate as would justify putting the bird in a more vulnerable category.<ref name=IUCN/>
